title.zh: 龙身人头、鼓腹作雷的雷泽之神
title.en: Dragon-Bodied God Who Drums His Belly for Thunder

summary.zh: 居于雷泽的神：龙的身体，人的头颅，一拍自己的肚腹便发出雷声。纬书相传华胥在雷泽踩到巨人足迹，感而生伏羲。
summary.en: A god of Lake Lei with a dragon's body and a human head who makes thunder by drumming his own belly. Apocryphal tradition says Huaxu trod in a giant footprint by this lake and conceived Fuxi.

description.zh:
《海内东经》记："雷泽中有雷神，龙身而人头，鼓其腹。在吴西。"雷泽在吴地以西，泽中住着雷神：修长的龙身顶着一颗人头，它不用锤，不用鼓，拍打自己的肚腹，腹音隆隆，就是人间听到的雷。《淮南子·墬形训》记"雷泽有神，龙身人头，鼓其腹而熙"，还添了一个"熙"字——它拍着肚子自得其乐，雷声原来是一位神的嬉戏。

这位雷神在感生神话里担任了要角。汉代纬书《诗含神雾》（《太平御览》引）说："大迹出雷泽，华胥履之，生宓牺。"巨大的足迹出现在雷泽岸边，姑娘华胥踩了上去，感应而孕，生下人首蛇身的伏羲。旧注多以为那足迹正是雷泽之神留下的，龙身的雷神因此被算作伏羲的父亲——中华人文始祖的血统里，有一半来自这位拍肚子打雷的神。后世雷公演变为鸟喙持椎、连击天鼓的形象，鼓腹作雷的古老想象则留在了这条经文里。
description.en:
The Classic of Regions Within the Seas: East places Lake Lei west of the land of Wu and gives it a resident: the thunder god, dragon-bodied and human-headed, who drums on his own belly. No hammer, no drumhead; the rumble of that belly is what men below hear as thunder. The Huainanzi repeats the description and adds one telling word: he drums his belly in play. Thunder, in this oldest version, is a god amusing himself.

The god was drafted into one of China's founding conception myths. The Han apocryphon Shi Han Shen Wu, quoted in the Taiping Yulan encyclopedia, relates that a giant footprint appeared by Lake Lei; the maiden Huaxu stepped into it and conceived Fuxi, the snake-bodied first sage who would devise the trigrams. Commentators took the footprint to be the lake-god's own, which makes the belly-drumming thunderer Fuxi's father. Later ages gave China's thunder deity a bird's beak, a mallet, and a ring of sky-drums; the older image of thunder as a dragon slapping its own stomach survives only in lines like this one.

value.en: Drums his belly; the rumble is thunder

value.zh: 旧说华胥履其迹而生伏羲
value.en: Fuxi, conceived from his footprint by Huaxu

sources.en: Shanhaijing, 'Classic of Regions Within the Seas: East'
Shi Han Shen Wu, quoted in the Taiping Yulan

volume.en: Classic of Regions Within the Seas: East
